name: Catherine "Cat" Morales
chief_complaint: I have the worst headache of my entire life, it hit me like a thunderbolt. I feel like my head is going to explode.
onset_description: Sudden, explosive onset of severe headache while straining during a bowel movement approximately 2 hours ago. Described as instantaneous, reaching maximum intensity within seconds. Associated with nausea, vomiting, neck stiffness, and photophobia. No preceding aura or warning signs.

headache.description: It's the worst pain I have ever felt in my life, it just slammed into me all at once. It feels like my head is being crushed from the inside out.
headache.onset: 2 hours ago, instantaneous maximum intensity (thunderclap onset)
headache.location: diffuse, worst at the back of the head and neck
headache.radiation: down the back of the neck
alleviating_factors: nothing - tried ibuprofen at home, no relief

neck_stiffness.description: My neck is so stiff I can't bend it forward, it makes the headache way worse when I try

vomiting.description: I've thrown up three times since it started, the headache is so bad it makes me vomit

photophobia.description: The light is killing me, please turn it off, it makes everything worse

nausea.description: I feel constantly nauseous, can't escape it

labs.BMP: Na 138, K 4.1, Cr 0.7, Glucose 128, BUN 14 - within normal limits

past_medical_history: Migraines (occasional, but patient clearly states this is completely different from any migraine)
Polycystic kidney disease (autosomal dominant, monitored by nephrologist)
medications: Sumatriptan 50mg as needed for migraines (last used 3 months ago)

social_history.smoking: Quit 10 years ago, smoked half a pack per day for 12 years
social_history.alcohol: Glass of wine 2-3 times per week
social_history.occupation: Hospital billing coordinator (works at this hospital)
social_history.living_situation: Divorced, lives alone in a condo. Adult daughter lives 20 minutes away.
social_history.exercise: Zumba classes 3 times per week, walks on off days
social_history.diet: Generally healthy, cooks most meals at home, watches sodium intake

conditions.father: Polycystic kidney disease, died at 55 from ruptured cerebral aneurysm
conditions.brother: Polycystic kidney disease, brain aneurysm (clipped at age 42)

personality: terrified, keeps eyes closed, speaks in a whisper because talking loudly worsens the headache. Usually a composed, organized professional but is visibly shaken. Keeps saying 'something is really wrong.'
communication_style: Speaks softly and minimally due to pain. Answers questions but keeps responses brief. Very insistent that this headache is 'nothing like my migraines.' When asked about family history, becomes tearful and says 'my dad died from a brain bleed.' Needs a darkened, quiet room for assessment.
pain_description_style: Dramatic but accurate - 'it felt like a bomb went off in my head.' Rates it 10/10 without hesitation. Clutches her head. Cries quietly. Says 'I've had migraines my whole life, this is completely different - this one could kill me.'

critical_findings: Thunderclap headache (instantaneous maximum intensity) - pathognomonic for SAH until proven otherwise
Worst headache of life in a patient who knows her baseline migraines
Meningeal signs: nuchal rigidity and photophobia
Family history of cerebral aneurysm rupture (father died of SAH, brother had aneurysm)
Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease - strong association with intracranial aneurysms (8-12% prevalence)
Onset with Valsalva (straining at stool) - classic aneurysm rupture trigger
No focal neurological deficits currently (Hunt-Hess Grade II)
recommended_interventions: STAT non-contrast CT head (sensitivity >95% within 6 hours for SAH)
If CT negative: lumbar puncture to evaluate for xanthochromia
CT angiography of head to identify aneurysm source
Blood pressure control (target SBP < 160 with nicardipine or labetalol drip)
IV analgesics for pain (avoid aspirin/NSAIDs, use acetaminophen and opioids)
Strict bed rest with head of bed elevated 30 degrees
Nimodipine 60mg every 4 hours (if SAH confirmed - prevents vasospasm)
Continuous cardiac monitoring (risk of neurogenic cardiac stunning)
